{{Short description|Attempt to influence the decision-making process within a specific group}}
{{Redirect2|Electioneering|Campaign trail|the Radiohead song|OK Computer{{!}}''OK Computer''|1984 board game|Campaign Trail: The Game of Presidential Elections{{!}}''Campaign Trail: The Game of Presidential Elections''}}
{{Globalize|date=December 2010}}
[[File:Lincoln button 1860.jpg|thumb|Presidential campaign button for [[Abraham Lincoln]], 1860.  The reverse side of the button shows a portrait of his running mate [[Hannibal Hamlin]].]]
{{Political campaigning}}

A '''political campaign''' is an organized effort which seeks to influence the decision making progress within a specific group. In [[democracy|democracies]], political campaigns often refer to [[election|electoral]] campaigns, by which representatives are chosen or [[referendum]]s are decided. In modern politics, the most high-profile political campaigns are focused on [[general elections]] and candidates for [[head of state]] or [[head of government]], often a [[President (government title)|president]] or [[prime minister]].

==Campaign message==
[[File:AMP supporters during rally on Balide, 1.jpg|thumb|Election campaign in [[East Timor]]: Truck Rally]]
The message of the campaign contains the ideas that the candidate wants to share with the voters.
It is to get those who agree with their ideas to support them when running for a political position.
The message often consists of several [[talking points]] about policy issues.
The points summarize the main ideas of the campaign and are repeated frequently in order to create a lasting impression with the voters. In many elections, the opposition party will try to get the candidate "off message" by bringing up policy or personal questions that are not related to the talking points. Most campaigns prefer to keep the message broad in order to attract the most potential voters.  A message that is too narrow can alienate voters or slow the candidate down with explaining details.
For example, in the [[2008 United States presidential election|2008 American presidential election]] [[John McCain]] originally used a message that focused on his patriotism and political experience: "Country First"; later the message was changed to shift attention to his role as "The Original Maverick" within the political establishment. [[Barack Obama]] ran on a consistent, simple message of "change" throughout his campaign.

According to a 2024 study, political practitioners in the United States poorly predict what kind of messaging is persuasive to the public.<ref name=":12">{{Cite journal |last1=Broockman |first1=David E. |last2=Kalla |first2=Joshua L. |last3=Caballero |first3=Christian |last4=Easton |first4=Matthew |date=2024 |title=Political practitioners poorly predict which messages persuade the public |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=121 |issue=45 |article-number=e2400076121 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2400076121 |doi-access=free |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=11551421 |pmid=39467135|bibcode=2024PNAS..12100076B }}</ref> The study found that practitioners performed barely better than chance at predicting persuasive effects; practitioners performed about as well as laypeople in predicting persuasive effects; and that practitioners' experience, expertise, information environment, and demographics did affect their accuracy.<ref name=":12" />

==Campaign finance==
{{Main|Campaign finance}}
Fundraising techniques include having the candidate call or meet with large donors, sending direct mail pleas to small donors, and courting interest groups who could end up spending millions on the race if it is significant to their interests.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Kaid |first1=Lynda Lee |title=Encyclopedia of political communication |last2=Holtz-Bacha |first2=Christina |date=2008 |publisher=Sage publ |isbn=978-1-4129-1799-5 |location=Los Angeles (Calif.)}}</ref> Funds can also funnel through something called a [[Political action committee|Political Action Committees]] (PACs) which the Supreme Court allowed in [[Citizens United v. FEC|Citizens United v. FEC (2010)]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ellerman |first=David |date=2025-04-25 |title=Are Corporations the Problem? |url=https://www.fdv.uni-lj.si/docs/default-source/tip/are-corporations-the-problem.pdf?sfvrsn=0 |journal=Teorija in Praksa |language=sl |pages=7–24 |doi=10.51936/tip.62.1.7 |issn=0040-3598}}</ref>

==Organization==
{{unreferenced section|date=September 2022}}
{{Main|Political campaign staff}}
In a modern political campaign, the campaign organization (or "machine") will have a coherent structure of personnel in the same manner as any business of similar size. The difference is it is a group paid employees and unpaid volunteers. Campaigns are run by [[campaign manager]]s, [[Political consulting|political consultants]], and [[Political Activist|activist]] or volunteers.

===Campaign manager===
{{Main|Campaign manager}}A campaign manager's primary duty is to ensure marketing campaigns achieve their objectives. They work with the marketing manager to create, execute and monitor the performance of campaigns and provide all the resources required to meet sales targets.<ref>{{cite book|title=Campaign Management : The Key Elements of a Campaign|url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003166375-8/campaign-management-anne-caprara|publisher=Taylor & Francis|date=2023-09-29|doi=10.4324/9781003166375-8|archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20240710170356/https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003166375-8/campaign-management-anne-caprara|archive-date=2024-07-10|language=en}}</ref> Campaign managers lead the way in fundraising, advertising, polling, and getting out to vote. They are the face of the campaign behind the candidate.

===Political consultants===
{{Main|Political consulting}}
Political consultants advise campaigns on virtually all of their activities, from research to field strategy. Consultants conduct candidate research, voter research, and [[opposition research]] for their clients. Their primary focus is mass media and controlling the image.

===Activists===
{{Main|Activism (politics)}}
In the context of political campaigns, activists are "foot soldiers" loyal to a campaign's cause. As supporters, they promote the campaign as volunteer activists. Such volunteers and interns may take part in activities such as [[canvassing]] door-to-door and making phone calls on behalf of the campaigns.

== The Campaign within the Electoral Cycle ==
Political campaigning is not a standalone event but a critical phase integrated into a complex, multi-step electoral cycle. The strategic landscape of any campaign is dictated by administrative categories that precede the actual trail, such as the definition of Electoral Boundaries and the legal framework for Political Party Registration. These factors, along with the accuracy of Voter Lists, determine the scope of voter targeting and resource allocation long before the first advertisement is aired.

The campaign's trajectory is further shaped by logistical elements, including Ballot Qualification and Election Security, which ensure a stable environment for candidate messaging. This process does not conclude on election day; it often transitions into the resolution of Electoral Complaints and Disputes, where the conduct of the campaign itself is adjudicated. By operating within this broader data-driven and administrative framework, a campaign functions as a vital mechanism within the overarching machinery of the election process.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Section 3: Key Election Process Categories {{!}} Open Election Data Initiative |url=https://openelectiondata.net/ |access-date=2026-04-13 |website=openelectiondata.net |language=en}}</ref>

=== Standards of Electoral Integrity ===
The legitimacy of a political campaign is anchored in the foundational principles of electoral integrity, transcending mere tactical success. A credible campaign environment is defined by inclusiveness, where systemic barriers are removed to ensure marginalized groups can participate without unreasonable restriction. This inclusivity is reinforced by transparency, requiring that every phase of the electoral cycle—from campaign financing to decision-making—remains open to independent scrutiny and the public’s right to information.<ref name=":13">{{Cite web |title=What are credible elections? {{!}} Open Election Data Initiative |url=https://openelectiondata.net/ |access-date=2026-04-13 |website=openelectiondata.net |language=en}}</ref>

The process relies on accountability, establishing a legal framework where candidates and administrative bodies are held responsible for their conduct through effective remedies and the rule of law. Ultimately, these elements foster genuine competitiveness, ensuring that political parties have equitable access to the ballot and that the electorate can engage in the marketplace of ideas free from intimidation or illegal influence. When these standards are met, the campaign functions not just as a race for office, but as a vital mechanism that truly reflects the collective will of the people.<ref name=":13" />

==Techniques==
A campaign team (which may be as small as one inspired individual, or a heavily resourced group of professionals) must consider how to [[political communication|communicate]] the message of the campaign, recruit volunteers, and raise money. Campaign advertising draws on techniques from commercial [[advertising]] and [[propaganda]], also entertainment and public relations, a mixture dubbed [[politainment]]. The avenues available to political campaigns when distributing their messages is limited by the law, available resources, and the imagination of the campaigns' participants. These techniques are often combined into a formal strategy known as the [[campaign plan]]. The plan takes account of a campaign's goal, message, target audience, and resources available. The campaign will typically seek to identify supporters at the same time as getting its message across.
The modern, open campaign method was pioneered by [[Aaron Burr]] during the [[1800 United States presidential election|American presidential election of 1800]].<ref>{{cite journal |author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |title=The Election of 1800 |url=https://lehrmaninstitute.org/history/1800.html#burr |journal=Lehrman Institute }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Unger |first=Harlow Giles |date=2014 |title=John Marshall: the Chief Justice Who Saved the Nation |publisher=Hachette Books |isbn=978-0-306-82221-6 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Wheelan |first=Joseph |date=2006 |title=Jefferson's Vendetta: the Pursuit of Aaron Burr and the Judiciary |publisher=PublicAffairs |isbn=0-7867-1689-4 }}</ref>

Another modern campaign method by political scientist Joel Bradshaw points out four key propositions for developing a successful campaign strategy. "First, in any election the electorate can be divided into three groups: the candidate's base, the opponent's base, and the undecided. Second, past election results, data from registered voter lists, and survey research make it possible to determine which people fall into each of these three groups. Third, it is neither possible nor necessary to get the support of all people. Fourth, and last, once a campaign has identified how to win, it can act to create the circumstances to bring about this victory. In order to succeed, campaigns should direct campaign resources – money, time, and message – to key groups of potential voters and nowhere else."<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Sides |first1=John |author1-link=John M. Sides |title=Campaigns and elections: rules, reality, strategy, choice |last2=Shaw |first2=Daron R. |last3=Grossmann |first3=Matthew |last4=Lipsitz |first4=Keena |year=2018 |isbn=978-0-393-64053-3 |edition=Third |location=New York |oclc=1027769548}}</ref>

===Campaign communication===
{{Main|Research strategies of election campaign communication research}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=June 2023}}
Election campaign communication refers to [[Research strategies of election campaign communication research#The scope of election campaign communication research|party-controlled communication]], e.g. [[campaign advertising]], and [[Research strategies of election campaign communication research#The scope of election campaign communication research|party-uncontrolled communication]], e.g. media coverage of elections. It is the strategy used to sway public opinion and gather support for a candidate.

===Campaign advertising===
{{Main|Campaign advertising}}

{{Unreferenced section|date=June 2023}}
Campaign advertising is the use of paid media (newspapers, radio, television, etc.) to influence the decisions made for and by groups. The ads are designed by [[political consulting|political consultants]] and the [[political campaign staff|campaign's staff]] as it is part of their role. The use of campaign advertising varies around the world. In the United States its a free market but in some countries like Ireland its forbidden to advertise campaigns.

===Media management===
Media management refers to the ability of a political campaign to control the message that it broadcasts to the public. The forms of media used in political campaigns can be classified into two distinct categories: "paid media" or "earned media".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Wynne|first=Robert|title=The Real Difference Between PR And Advertising|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/robertwynne/2014/07/08/the-real-difference-between-pr-and-advertising-credibility/|access-date=2021-09-27|website=Forbes|language=en}}</ref> There are times where some campaigns get little attention, but the ones that do get highlighted for the effectiveness and dramatic events. In the book ''Campaigns and Elections'' by author John Sides, it says, "For those that do get attention, media coverage often emphasizes what is new, dramatic, or scandalous. Unlike the candidates themselves, the news media or at least those outlets that strive for objectivity are not seeking to manipulate citizens into voting for their particular candidates." (Sides 2018).{{citation needed|date=September 2024}}

Paid media refers to any media attention that is directly generated from spending.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Definition of Paid Media - Gartner Marketing Glossary|url=https://www.gartner.com/en/marketing/glossary/paid-media|access-date=2021-09-27|website=Gartner|language=en}}</ref> This form of media is commonly found through political advertisements and organized events. An advantage of paid media is that it allows political campaigns to tailor the messages they show the public and control when the public sees them. Campaigns often prioritize spending in contested regions and increase their paid media expenses as an election approaches.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Adgate|first=Brad|title=The 2020 Elections Will Set (Another) Ad Spending Record|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/bradadgate/2019/09/03/the-2020-elections-will-set-another-ad-spending-record/|access-date=2021-10-04|website=Forbes|language=en}}</ref> Electoral campaigns often conclude with a "closing argument ad", an advertisement that summarizes the campaign's core themes and explains the candidate's vision for the future.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Closing argument (political campaigns)|url=https://ballotpedia.org/Closing_argument_(political_campaigns)|access-date=2021-10-04|website=Ballotpedia|language=en}}</ref> In the 2020 US presidential election, Joe Biden's "Rising" ad starts with him saying "we're in a battle for the soul of this nation" and a worker in Donald Trump's Pennsylvania ad stated "that will be the end of my job and thousands of others" if Trump lost.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Gringlas|first=Sam|date=2020-10-27|title=Biden And Trump Campaigns Stress Closing Themes In New TV Ads|language=en|work=NPR|url=https://www.npr.org/2020/10/27/928258916/biden-and-trump-campaigns-stress-closing-themes-in-new-tv-ads|access-date=2021-10-04}}</ref>

Earned media describes free media coverage, often from news stories or social media posts.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Don|first=Stacks|title=A Professional and Practitioner's Guide to Public Relations Research, Measurement, and Evaluation}}</ref> Unlike paid media, earned media does not incur an expense to the campaign. Earned media does not imply that the political campaign is mentioned in a positive manner. Political campaigns may often receive earned media from gaffes or scandals. In the 2016 United States Presidential Election, a majority of the media coverage surrounding Hillary Clinton was focused on her scandals, with the most prevalent topics being topics related to her emails.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2019-12-17|title=Partisanship, Propaganda, and Disinformation: Online Media and the 2016 U.S. Presidential Election {{!}} Berkman Klein Center|url=https://cyber.harvard.edu/publications/2017/08/mediacloud|access-date=2021-09-27|website=cyber.harvard.edu|language=en}}</ref>

Experts say that effective media management is an essential component of a successful political campaign. Studies show that candidates with higher media attention tend to have greater success in elections.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=van Erkel|first1=Patrick F. A.|last2=Van Aelst|first2=Peter|last3=Thijssen|first3=Peter|date=2020-04-01|title=Does media attention lead to personal electoral success? Differences in long and short campaign media effects for top and ordinary political candidates|journal=Acta Politica|language=en|volume=55|issue=2|pages=156–174|doi=10.1057/s41269-018-0109-x|hdl=10067/1539890151162165141 |s2cid=150155578 |issn=1741-1416|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Each form of media can influence the other. Paid media may raise the newsworthiness of an event which could lead to an increase in earned media.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rinallo|first1=Diego|last2=Basuroy|first2=Suman|date=2009|title=Does Advertising Spending Influence Media Coverage of the Advertiser?|journal=Journal of Marketing|volume=73|issue=6|pages=33–46|doi=10.1509/jmkg.73.6.33 |jstor=20619057 |s2cid=27883769 |issn=0022-2429}}</ref> Campaigns may also spend money to emphasize stories circulating through media networks. Research suggests that neither form of media is inherently superior. A 2009 study found that media coverage was not significantly more effective than paid advertisements.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Don|first=Stacks|date=June 2009|title=Exploring the Comparative Communications Effectiveness of Advertising and Public Relations: A Replication and Extension of Prior Experiments|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/255625596}}</ref>

{{anchor|Campaign rally|Rally}}
<!-- [[Campaign rally]] links here -->

===Demonstrations===
{{Main|Political demonstration}}

{{empty section|date=December 2024}}
[[File:Photograph of First Lady Betty Ford Campaigning for President Gerald Ford at a Chinese-American Rally in Chinatown... - NARA - 186837.tif|thumb|right|A political rally in [[Chinatown, Los Angeles]], featuring [[Betty Ford]] campaigning for her husband, U.S. President [[Gerald Ford]], during the [[1976 United States presidential election|1976 presidential campaign]]]]

===Modern technology and the internet===
{{Main|Internet activism}}

The internet is now a core element of modern political campaigns. Communication technologies such as e-mail, websites, and [[podcasts]] for various forms of activism enable faster communications by citizen movements and deliver a message to a large audience. These [[Internet]] technologies are used for cause-related fundraising, lobbying, volunteering, community building, and organizing. Individual political candidates are also using the internet to promote their election campaign. In a study of Norwegian election campaigns, politicians reported they used social media for marketing and for dialogue with voters. Facebook was the primary platform for marketing and Twitter was used for more continuous dialogue.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Enli|first=Sara Gunn|author2=Skogerbø, Eli|title=Personalized campaigns in party-centred politics|journal=Information, Communication & Society|year=2013|volume=16|issue=5|page=757|doi=10.1080/1369118x.2013.782330|s2cid=143140185}}</ref>

Signifying the importance of internet political campaigning, Barack Obama's presidential campaign relied heavily on [[social media]], Search Engine Optimization (SEO) and [[new media]] channels to engage voters, recruit campaign volunteers, and raise [[campaign fund]]s. The campaign brought the spotlight on the importance of using internet in new-age political campaigning by utilizing various forms of social media and new media (including Facebook, YouTube and a custom generated social engine) to reach new target populations. The campaign's social website, my.BarackObama.com, utilized a low cost and efficient method of mobilizing voters and increasing participation among various voter populations.<ref>{{cite web | url = http://www.newsweek.com/2008/11/21/president-2-0.html | title = President 2.0 | access-date = 2010-05-11 | last = Lyons | first = Daniel | date = 2008-11-22 | work = Newsweek | quote = Obama harnessed the grass-roots power of the Web to get elected. How will he use that power now?}}</ref> This new media was incredibly successful at reaching the younger population while helping all populations organize and promote action. In the book Campaigns and Elections author John sides also speaks upon this on page 235 and says, "Online communities can still promote involvement in campaigns: large experiments on Facebook found that users who saw that their Facebook friends had reported voting were themselves a bit more likely to turn out to vote. But there is also evidence that offering token public support for a cause on Facebook or Twitter may make one less likely to be involved in offline campaign activities" (Sides 2018).

Now, online election campaign information can be shared in a rich information format through campaign landing pages, integrating Google's rich snippets, structured data,<ref name="google.com">{{cite web|url=https://developers.google.com/search/docs/guides/intro-structured-data|title=Introduction to Structured Data - Search}}</ref> [[social media]] [[Open Graph|open graphs]], and [[husting]] support file formats for YouTube like  [[SubRip|.sbv]], {{abbr|.srt|subtitle resource track}}, and {{abbr|.vtt|video text trace}}. High proficiency and effective algorithmic integration will be the core factor in the framework. This technology integration helps campaign information reach a wide audience in split-seconds. This was successfully tested and implemented in the 2015 [[Aruvikkara]] election and the [[2020 Kerala local elections|2020 Kerala elections]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=ഡെസ്ക്|first=വെബ്|date=2020-11-18|title=ആരും വോട്ടുചെയ്തുപോകും; ഡിജിറ്റൽ പ്ലാറ്റ്ഫോമുകളിൽ പ്രചാരണം കളർഫുൾ {{!}} Madhyamam|url=https://www.madhyamam.com/editors-choice/colourful-election-campaign-in-digital-platforms-602675|access-date=2021-02-23|website=www.madhyamam.com|language=en}}</ref> Marcus Giavanni, social media consultant, blockchain developer and second place opponent in the [[2015 Denver mayoral election]], was first to file for the [[2019 Denver mayoral election|2019 election]]. Marcus Giavanni used advanced algorithms, artificial intelligence, and voice indexing predictions to box in campaigns.<ref name="auto1">{{cite news|url=https://www.denverpost.com/2018/05/29/denver-mayor-candidates-2019/|title=Several candidates have filed to run for Denver mayor in 2019. Here's what they say.|date=May 29, 2018|newspaper=[[Denver Post]]|access-date=October 2, 2018}}</ref>

===Husting===
{{Main|Husting}}

A [[husting]], or the hustings, was originally a physical platform from which representatives presented their views or cast votes before a parliamentary or other election body. By [[wiktionary:husting|metonymy]], the term may now refer to any event, such as debates or speeches, during an election campaign where one or more of the representative candidates are present. In modern terms it means the campaign trail in [[Canada]] and Britain.<ref>{{Cite news|title=What makes a great political speech? Let's talk about oratory, my fellow citizens|url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2022/may/19/what-makes-a-great-political-speech-lets-talk-about-oratory-my-fellow-citizens|work=The Guardian|date=2022-05-18|access-date=2025-12-01|issn=0261-3077|language=en-GB|first=Paul|last=Daley}}</ref>

===Other techniques===
[[Image:Layton and Duceppe baby-kissing.jpg|thumb|[[New Democratic Party (Canada)|NDP]] leader [[Jack Layton]] and [[Bloc Québécois]] leader [[Gilles Duceppe]] greet babies - a traditional campaign activity - at the [[Fête nationale du Québec]] in [[Montreal]]]]

*Writing directly to members of the public (either via a professional marketing firm or, particularly on a small scale, by volunteers)
*By distributing leaflets or selling newspapers
*[[Election poster]]s
*[[Canvassing|Phone Banking]]
*Through websites, online communities, and solicited or unsolicited bulk email<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.campaigntv.org|title=Campaign TV|access-date=2020-05-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170202143542/http://www.campaigntv.org/|archive-date=2017-02-02}}</ref>
*Through a new technique known as [[microtargeting]] that helps identify and target small demographic slices of voters
*Through a [[whistlestop tour]] - a series of brief appearances in several small towns
*Hampering the ability of political competitors to campaign, by such techniques as counter-rallies, picketing of rival parties' meetings, or overwhelming rival candidates' offices with mischievous phone calls (most political parties in representative democracies publicly distance themselves from such disruptive and morale-affecting tactics, with the exception of those parties self-identifying as [[activist]])
* Using endorsements of other celebrated party members to boost support (see [[coattail effect]])
* Using a campaign surrogate - a celebrity or person of influence, campaigning on a candidate's behalf
* Remaining close to or at home to make speeches to supporters who come to visit as part of a [[front porch campaign]]
* Vote-by-mail, previously known as "absentee ballots", have grown significantly in importance as an election tool. Campaigns in most states must have a strategy in place to impact early voting.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Myers|first=Elise|date=2020-09-11|title=What is one effective strategy used by political campaigns?|language=en|work=Campaigning Info|url=https://campaigninginfo.com/one-effective-strategy-used-by-political-campaigns/|access-date=2021-04-12}}</ref>
* Sale of official campaign merchandise (colloquially known as [[Promotional merchandise|swag]], in reference to the baiting technique) as a way of commuting a competitor's popularity into campaign donations, volunteer recruitment, and free advertising<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/off-the-bus-reporter/obama-campaign-rewrites-f_b_140616.html |title=Off The Bus: Obama Campaign Rewrites Fundraising Rules by Selling Merchandise |date=3 November 2008 |work=Huffington Post |access-date=20 May 2009 |author=Diane Tucker, Dawn Teo}}</ref>

==Campaign types==

===Informational campaign===
[[File:Political campaign UK-London.jpg |180px|thumb|right|A [[Conservative Party (UK)|Conservative]]-led political campaign brochure for the [[Mayor of London]]]]
An informational campaign is a political campaign designed to raise public awareness and support for the positions of a candidate (or her/his party).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sisr.net/publications/0706fletcher.pdf|title=Publications - Social Research - Swinburne University - Melbourne|access-date=2008-08-04|archive-date=2014-02-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140210165702/http://www.sisr.net/publications/0706fletcher.pdf}}</ref> It is more intense than a paper campaign, which consists of little more than filing the necessary papers to get on the ballot, but is less intense than a competitive campaign, which aims to actually win election to the office. An informational campaign typically focuses on low-cost outreach such as news releases, getting interviewed in the paper, making a brochure for door to door distribution, organizing poll workers, etc.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.lppa.org/component/option,com_smf/Itemid,60/topic,9.0/ |title=Changing Focus/Priorities |publisher=Lppa.org |access-date=2008-11-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080725075624/http://www.lppa.org/component/option,com_smf/Itemid,60/topic,9.0/ |archive-date=July 25, 2008 }}</ref>

===Paper campaign===
A paper campaign is a political campaign in which the candidate only files the necessary paperwork to appear on the [[ballot]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dullardmush.blogspot.com/2006/10/10-questions-with-libertarian-party-of_18.html|title=10 Questions With Libertarian Party of Nevada Chairman Jim Duensing, Pt. 2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.lpillinois.org/news/newsletters/LPI2002Issue4.pdf | title=Media | access-date=2015-04-02 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171019234459/http://www.lpillinois.org/news/newsletters/LPI2002Issue4.pdf | archive-date=2017-10-19 }}</ref> The purpose of such a token effort may be simply to increase name awareness of a minor political party, to give voters of a certain ideology an opportunity to vote accordingly, or to ensure that the party has candidates in every constituency. It can be a cost-effective means of attracting media coverage. An informational campaign, by contrast, may involve news releases, newspaper interviews, door-to-door campaigning, and organizing polls. As the level of seriousness rises, the marginal cost of reaching more people rises accordingly, due to the high cost of TV commercials, paid staff, etc. which are used by competitive campaigns.<ref>[http://www.lppa.org/component/option,com_smf/Itemid,60/topic,9.0/ Changing Focus/Priorities<!-- Bot generated title -->] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080725075624/http://www.lppa.org/component/option,com_smf/Itemid,60/topic,9.0/ |date=July 25, 2008 }}</ref> Paper candidates do not expect to be elected and usually run simply as a way of helping the more general campaign. However, an unexpected surge in support for the party may result in many paper candidates being unexpectedly elected, as for example happened to the [[New Democratic Party]] in Quebec during the [[Canadian federal election, 2011|2011 federal election]].

== Effects ==
A 2018 study in the ''American Political Science Review'' found that campaigns have "an average effect of zero in general elections".<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal |last1=Kalla |first1=Joshua |last2=Broockman |first2=David E. |date=2018 |title=The Minimal Persuasive Effects of Campaign Contact in General Elections: Evidence from 49 Field Experiments |journal=American Political Science Review|volume=112 |pages=148–166 |doi=10.1017/S0003055417000363 |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/103775sx }}</ref><ref name=":10">{{Cite news|url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2017/9/28/16367580/campaigning-doesnt-work-general-election-study-kalla-broockman|title=A massive new study reviews the evidence on whether campaigning works. The answer's bleak.|work=Vox|access-date=2017-10-04}}</ref> The study found two instances where campaigning was effective: "First, when candidates take unusually unpopular positions and campaigns invest unusually heavily in identifying persuadable voters. Second, when campaigns contact voters long before election day and measure effects immediately — although this early persuasion decays."<ref name=":9" /><ref name=":10" />

One reason why it is hard to judge the effectiveness of an election campaign is because many people know who they want to vote for long before the campaigns are started. Voters are more likely to vote for a nominee based on whose values align closest with theirs. Studies suggest that party flips come from the analysis of how a voter sees their parties performance in the years before a campaign even begins.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Campbell|first=James E.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wjtuDwAAQBAJ&q=election+campaign+history+in+america&pg=PR9|title=The American Campaign, Second Edition: U.S. Presidential Campaigns and the National Vote|date=2008-01-14|publisher=Texas A&M University Press|isbn=978-1-58544-628-5|language=en}}</ref>

Another study suggests that at the [[2017 Austrian legislative election]], 31% of voters admitted to either developing of changing their party preferences during the election campaign. The study provides data that shows how the main parties within Austria had differing levels of voters flipping toward them, thus proving that an election campaign has some level of effectiveness that differs between parties, depending on factors such as media presence.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Johann|first1=David|last2=Königslöw|first2=Katharina Kleinen-von|last3=Kritzinger|first3=Sylvia|last4=Thomas|first4=Kathrin|date=2018-04-03|title=Intra-Campaign Changes in Voting Preferences: The Impact of Media and Party Communication|url= |journal=Political Communication|language=en|volume=35|issue=2|pages=261–286|doi=10.1080/10584609.2017.1339222|issn=1058-4609|pmc=5894360|pmid=29695892}}</ref>

=== Spending ===
In presidential campaigns in the United States, research indicates that a $10 million advantage in spending in an individual states leads to approximately 27,000 more votes for the campaign in the state, which can be sufficient to win a close race.<ref name=":11">{{Cite journal |last1=Sides |first1=John |last2=Vavreck |first2=Lynn |last3=Warshaw |first3=Christopher |date=2022 |title=The Effect of Television Advertising in United States Elections |journal=American Political Science Review |language=en |volume=116 |issue=2 |page=710 |doi=10.1017/S000305542100112X |s2cid=232333920 |issn=0003-0554|doi-access=free }}</ref> In down-ballot races, spending matters more. Scholars have estimated that a $2 million advantage can net a Senate campaign 10,000 votes.<ref name=":11" />

=== Presidential campaigns ===
A large body of political science research emphasizes how "fundamentals" – the state of the economy, whether the country is at war, how long the president's party has held the office, and which candidate is more ideologically moderate – predict presidential election outcomes.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Gelman|first1=Andrew|last2=King|first2=Gary|date=2008-01-17|title=Why are American Presidential Election Campaign Polls so Variable When Votes are so Predictable?|ssrn=1084120|location=Rochester, NY|publisher=Social Science Research Network|journal=British Journal of Political Science}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Noel|first=Hans|title=Ten Things Political Scientists Know that You Don't|journal=The Forum|volume=8|issue=3|doi=10.2202/1540-8884.1393|year=2010|article-number=0000102202154088841393 |s2cid=145700304}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bartels|first1=Larry M.|last2=Zaller|first2=John|date=2001-03-01|title=Presidential Vote Models: A Recount|journal=PS: Political Science & Politics|volume=34|issue=1|pages=9–20|doi=10.1017/S1049096501000026|issn=1537-5935|citeseerx=10.1.1.471.3300|s2cid=154297918}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Hibbs|first=Douglas A. Jr.|date=2000-07-01|title=Bread and Peace Voting in U.S. Presidential Elections|journal=Public Choice|language=en|volume=104|issue=1–2|pages=149–180|doi=10.1023/A:1005292312412|s2cid=11065014|issn=0048-5829}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=http://press.princeton.edu/titles/8937.html|title=Vavreck, L.: The Message Matters: The Economy and Presidential Campaigns. (eBook and Paperback)|website=press.princeton.edu|date=26 July 2009|isbn=978-0-691-13963-0|access-date=2016-04-23|last1=Vavreck|first1=Lynn|publisher=Princeton University Press }}</ref> However, campaigns may be necessary to enlighten otherwise uninformed voters about the fundamentals, which thus become increasingly predictive of preferences as the campaign progresses.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite book|url=http://press.uchicago.edu/ucp/books/book/chicago/T/bo13948250.html|title=The Timeline of Presidential Elections|series=Chicago Studies in American Politics |publisher=University of Chicago Press |access-date=2016-04-23}}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite book|url=https://global.oup.com/academic/product/the-obama-victory-9780195399554?cc=is&lang=en&|title=The Obama Victory|website=global.oup.com|date=26 August 2010|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-539955-4|access-date=2016-04-23}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite book|url=http://press.princeton.edu/titles/10350.html|title=Sides, J. and Vavreck, L.: The Gamble: Choice and Chance in the 2012 Presidential Election. (Updated Edition) (eBook and Paperback)|website=press.princeton.edu|date=7 September 2014|isbn=978-0-691-16363-5|access-date=2016-04-23|last1=Sides|first1=John|last2=Vavreck|first2=Lynn|publisher=Princeton University Press }}</ref> Research suggests that "the 2012 presidential campaigns increased turnout in highly targeted states by 7–8 percentage points, on average, indicating that modern campaigns can significantly alter the size and composition of the voting population".<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Enos|first1=Ryan D.|last2=Fowler|first2=Anthony|date=2016-05-01|title=Aggregate Effects of Large-Scale Campaigns on Voter Turnout|journal=Political Science Research and Methods|volume=FirstView|issue=4|pages=733–751|doi=10.1017/psrm.2016.21|s2cid=155467129|issn=2049-8489}}</ref>

==== National conventions ====
A consensus in the political science literature holds that national conventions usually have a measurable effect on presidential elections that is relatively resistant to decay.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" />

==== Presidential and vice-presidential debates ====
Research is mixed on the precise impact of debates.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Shaw|first=Daron R.|date=1999-05-01|title=A Study of Presidential Campaign Event Effects from 1952 to 1992|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/article_S0022381600054578|journal=The Journal of Politics|volume=61|issue=2|pages=387–422|doi=10.2307/2647509|issn=1468-2508|jstor=2647509|s2cid=154687906|url-access=subscription}}{{Dead link|date=May 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> Rather than encourage viewers to update their political views in accordance with the most persuasive arguments, viewers instead update their views to merely reflect what their favored candidate is saying.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Abramowitz|first=Alan I.|date=1978-01-01|title=The Impact of a Presidential Debate on Voter Rationality|jstor=2110467|journal=American Journal of Political Science|volume=22|issue=3|pages=680–690|doi=10.2307/2110467}}</ref>

==== Presidential primaries ====
The fundamentals matter less in the outcome of presidential primaries. One prominent theory holds that the outcome of presidential primaries is largely determined by the preferences of party elites.<ref>{{Cite book|url=http://press.uchicago.edu/ucp/books/book/chicago/P/bo5921600.html|title=The Party Decides|series=Chicago Studies in American Politics |publisher=University of Chicago Press |access-date=2016-04-23}}</ref> Presidential primaries are therefore less predictive, as various types of events may impact elites' perception of the viability of candidates. Gaffes, debates and media narratives play a greater role in primaries than in presidential elections.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://themonkeycage.org/2012/10/why-news-coverage-of-the-debate-may-matter-more-than-the-debate/|title=Why News Coverage of the Debate May Matter More than the Debate |last = Sides|first = John|website=The Monkey Cage|date=2 October 2012|language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Strategies ===
[[File:Londonderry, New Hampshire town election electioneering.jpg|thumb|Electioneering in front of a voting location for municipal elections in [[New Hampshire]] in 2022]]
Traditional ground campaigning and voter contacts remain the most effective strategies.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal|title = Polls and Elections: Campaign Field Offices and Voter Mobilization in 2012|journal = Presidential Studies Quarterly|date = 2015-09-01|issn = 1741-5705|pages = 573–580|volume = 45|issue = 3|doi = 10.1111/psq.12213|language = en|first = Aaron C.|last = Weinschenk}}</ref> Some research suggests that knocking on doors can increase turnout by as much as 10%<ref>{{Cite journal|title = The Effects of Canvassing, Telephone Calls, and Direct Mail on Voter Turnout: A Field Experiment|jstor = 2585837|journal = The American Political Science Review|date = 2000-01-01|pages = 653–663|volume = 94|issue = 3|doi = 10.2307/2585837|first1 = Alan S.|last1 = Gerber|first2 = Donald P.|last2 = Green|s2cid = 59034723|url = https://semanticscholar.org/paper/f7a224707f4dfa53058adcfcae2b44ed4a29686e}}</ref> and phone calls by as much as 4%.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Quality Is Job One: Professional and Volunteer Voter Mobilization Calls|journal = American Journal of Political Science|date = 2007-04-01|issn = 1540-5907|pages = 269–282|volume = 51|issue = 2|doi = 10.1111/j.1540-5907.2007.00250.x|language = en|first = David W.|last = Nickerson}}</ref> One study suggests that lawn signs increase vote share by 1.7 percentage points.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Green|first1=Donald P.|last2=Krasno|first2=Jonathan S.|last3=Coppock|first3=Alexander|last4=Farrer|first4=Benjamin D.|last5=Lenoir|first5=Brandon|last6=Zingher|first6=Joshua N.|date=2016-03-01|title=The effects of lawn signs on vote outcomes: Results from four randomized field experiments|journal=Electoral Studies|volume=41|pages=143–150|doi=10.1016/j.electstud.2015.12.002|s2cid=59042821 }}</ref> A review of more than 200 get-out-the-vote experiments finds that the most effective tactics are personal: Door-to-door canvassing increases turnout by an average of about 2.5 percentage points; volunteer phone calls raise it by about 1.9 points, compared to 1.0 points for calls from commercial phone banks; automated phone messages are ineffective.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Field Experiments and the Study of Voter Turnout|journal = Journal of Elections, Public Opinion and Parties|date = 2013-02-01|issn = 1745-7289|pages = 27–48|volume = 23|issue = 1|doi = 10.1080/17457289.2012.728223|first1 = Donald P.|last1 = Green|first2 = Mary C.|last2 = McGrath|first3 = Peter M.|last3 = Aronow|s2cid = 53376573}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Jacobson|first=Gary C.|date=2015-01-01|title=How Do Campaigns Matter?|journal=Annual Review of Political Science|volume=18|issue=1|pages=31–47|doi=10.1146/annurev-polisci-072012-113556|doi-access=free}}</ref>  Using out-of-state volunteers for canvassing is less effective in increasing turnout than using local and trained volunteers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sinclair |first1=Betsy |last2=McConnell |first2=Margaret |last3=Michelson |first3=Melissa R. |author-link3=Melissa R. Michelson |date=2013-01-01 |title=Local Canvassing: The Efficacy of Grassroots Voter Mobilization |journal=Political Communication |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=42–57 |doi=10.1080/10584609.2012.737413 |issn=1058-4609 |s2cid=42874299}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Middleton |first1=Joel A. |last2=Green |first2=Donald P. |date=March 2008 |title=Do community-based voter mobilization campaigns work even in battleground states? Evaluating the effectiveness of MoveOn's 2004 outreach campaign |journal=[[Quarterly Journal of Political Science]] |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=63–82 |doi=10.1561/100.00007019 |ssrn=1316927 |s2cid=52044755}}</ref>

There are many different types of strategies that are also used during these campaigns that target certain people and try to win them over. people are also paid to help get candidates to vote for a certain side. In the book ''Campaigns and Elections'', author John Sides says, "Campaigns involve a variety of actors. More visible are the candidates themselves. Their strategic choices involve every facet of a campaign: whether to run in the first place, what issues to emphasize, what specific messages or themes to discuss, which kinds of media to use, and which citizens to target."

According to political scientists [[Donald Green]] and Alan Gerber, it costs $31 to produce a vote going door to door, $91-$137 to produce a vote by sending out direct mailers, $47 per vote from leafletting, $58-$125 per vote from commercial phone banking, and $20-$35 per vote from voluntary phone banking.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://theworld.org/stories/2016/05/18/every-vote-matters-whats-best-way-get-them |title=Every vote matters. What's the best way to get them?|website=Public Radio International|date=18 May 2016 |language=en-US|access-date=2016-06-10}}</ref> A 2018 study in the ''[[The American Economic Review|American Economic Review]]'' found that door-to-door canvassing on behalf of the Francois Hollande campaign in the 2012 French presidential election "did not affect turnout, but increased Hollande's vote share in the first round and accounted for one fourth of his victory margin in the second. Visits' impact persisted in later elections, suggesting a lasting persuasion effect."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Vincent|first=Pons|title=Will a Five-Minute Discussion Change Your Mind? Countrywide Experiment on Voter Choice in France|journal=American Economic Review |language= en|issue=6|pages=1322–1363|doi=10.1257/aer.20160524|issn=0002-8282|year=2018|volume=108|doi-access=free}}</ref> According to a 2018 study, repeated get-out-the-vote phone calls had diminishing effects but each additional phone call increased the probability to vote by 0.6-1.0 percentage points.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Repeated treatment in a GOTV field experiment: Distinguishing between intensive and extensive margin effects}}</ref> Another 2018 study found that "party leaflets boost turnout by 4.3 percentage points while canvassing has a small additional effect (0.6 percentage points)" in a United Kingdom election.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Townsley|first=Joshua|date=3 October 2018|title=Is it worth door-knocking? Evidence from a United Kingdom-based Get Out The Vote (GOTV) field experiment on the effect of party leaflets and canvass visits on voter turnout|journal=Political Science Research and Methods|volume=13 |language=en|pages=21–35|doi=10.1017/psrm.2018.39|issn=2049-8470|doi-access=free}}</ref>

A 2016 study found that visits by a candidate to states have modest effects: "visits are most effective in influencing press coverage at the national level and within battleground states. Visits' effects on voters themselves, however, are much more modest than consultants often claim, and visits appear to have no effects outside the market that hosts a visit."<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last=Wood|first=Thomas|date=2016-09-01|title=What The Heck Are We Doing in Ottumwa, Anyway? Presidential Candidate Visits and Their Political Consequence|journal=The Annals of the American Academy of Political and Social Science|language=en|volume=667|issue=1|pages=110–125|doi=10.1177/0002716216661488|s2cid=151905273|issn=0002-7162}}</ref> The authors of the study argue that it would be more effective for campaigns to go to the pockets of the country where wealthy donors are (for fundraising) and hold rallies in the populous states both to attract national press and raise funds.<ref name=":8" /> A 2005 study found that campaign visits had no statistically significant effect, after controlling for other factors, on voter turnout in the 1992, 1996, and 2000 elections.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Holbrook|first1=Thomas M.|last2=McClurg|first2=Scott D.|date=2005-10-01|title=The Mobilization of Core Supporters: Campaigns, Turnout, and Electoral Composition in United States Presidential Elections|journal=American Journal of Political Science|language=en|volume=49|issue=4|pages=689–703|doi=10.1111/j.1540-5907.2005.00149.x|issn=1540-5907}}</ref> On the other hand, a 2017 paper of the 1948 presidential election provides "strong evidence that candidate visits can influence electoral returns".<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Heersink|first1=Boris|last2=Peterson|first2=Brenton D.|title=Truman defeats Dewey: The effect of campaign visits on election outcomes|journal=Electoral Studies|volume=49|pages=49–64|doi=10.1016/j.electstud.2017.07.007|year=2017}}</ref> Other research also provides evidence that campaign visits increase vote share.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Bednar|first=Steven|date=2017-07-28|title=Campaigning and election outcomes in a presidential primary election|journal=Applied Economics Letters|volume=25|issue=10|pages=713–717|doi=10.1080/13504851.2017.1360999|s2cid=158086367|issn=1350-4851}}</ref>

Campaigns may also rely on strategically placed field offices to acquire votes. The Obama 2008 campaign's extensive use of field offices has been credited as crucial to winning in the states of Indiana and North Carolina.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Darr |first1=Joshua P. |last2=Levendusky |first2=Matthew S. |date=2014-05-01 |title=Relying on the Ground Game The Placement and Effect of Campaign Field Offices |journal=American Politics Research |language=en |volume=42 |issue=3 |pages=529–548 |doi=10.1177/1532673X13500520 |issn=1532-673X |s2cid=154664815}}</ref> Each field office that the Obama campaign opened in 2012 gave him approximately a 0.3% greater vote share.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Masket |first1=Seth |last2=Sides |first2=John |last3=Vavreck |first3=Lynn |date=2015-07-27 |title=The Ground Game in the 2012 Presidential Election |journal=Political Communication |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=169–187 |doi=10.1080/10584609.2015.1029657 |issn=1058-4609 |s2cid=146490882}}</ref>  According to one study, the cost per vote by having a field office is $49.40.<ref name=":1" /> A 2024 study found "that campaign offices help candidates in small but meaningful ways, delivering modest but quantifiable increases in candidate vote share in the areas where they open... Field offices can increase candidate vote share, but their value differs across parties: Democrats benefit more in battleground states and populous areas, while Republicans' largely rural base of support in recent years provides challenges for maximizing the benefits of in-person organizing."<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Darr |first1=Joshua P. |last2=Whyard |first2=Sean |date=2024 |title=Storefront Campaigning |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/elements/storefront-campaigning/759490BAFD11277AEFDF466A142C8ED1 |journal=Cambridge University Press |language=en |doi=10.1017/9781009443357|isbn=978-1-009-44335-7 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

According to a 2020 study, campaign spending on messaging to voters affects voter support for candidates.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Schuster|first=Steven Sprick|date=2020-02-25|title=Does Campaign Spending Affect Election Outcomes? New Evidence from Transaction-Level Disbursement Data|journal=The Journal of Politics|volume=82|issue=4|pages=1502–1515|doi=10.1086/708646|s2cid=214168948|issn=0022-3816}}</ref> Another 2020 study found that political advertising had small effects regardless of context, message, sender, and receiver.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Coppock|first1=Alexander|last2=Hill|first2=Seth J.|last3=Vavreck|first3=Lynn|date=2020-09-01|title=The small effects of political advertising are small regardless of context, message, sender, or receiver: Evidence from 59 real-time randomized experiments|journal=Science Advances|language=en|volume=6|issue=36|article-number=eabc4046|doi=10.1126/sciadv.abc4046|pmid=32917601|pmc=7467695|bibcode=2020SciA....6.4046.|issn=2375-2548|doi-access=free}}</ref> A 2022 study found that voters are persuadable to switch support for candidates when they are exposed to new information.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Broockman |first1=David E. |last2=Kalla |first2=Joshua L. |date=2022 |title=When and Why Are Campaigns' Persuasive Effects Small? Evidence from the 2020 U.S. Presidential Election |journal=American Journal of Political Science |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=833–849 |language=en |doi=10.1111/ajps.12724 |issn=0092-5853|url=https://osf.io/m7326/ |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Political science research generally finds negative advertisement (which has increased over time)<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Dowling |first1=Conor M. |title=Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Politics |last2= Krupnikov |first2=Yanna |date=2016-11-22 |isbn=978-0-19-022863-7 |language=en |chapter=The Effects of Negative Advertising |doi=10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.013.51 |chapter-url= https://oxfordre.com/politics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780190228637.001.0001/acrefore-9780190228637-e-51}}</ref> to be ineffective both at reducing the support and turnout for the opponent.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lau |first1=Richard R. |last2=Sigelman |first2=Lee |last3=Rovner |first3=Ivy Brown |date=2007-11-01 |title=The Effects of Negative Political Campaigns: A Meta-Analytic Reassessment |journal=Journal of Politics |language=en |volume=69 |issue=4 |pages=1176–1209 |doi=10.1111/j.1468-2508.2007.00618.x |issn=1468-2508 |s2cid=155001726}}</ref> A 2021 study in the ''American Political Science Review'' found that television campaign ads do affect election outcomes, in particular in down-ballot races.<ref name=":11" /> According to political scientists Stephen Ansolabehere and Shanto Iyengar, negative ads do succeed at driving down overall turnout though.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book |last1=Ansolabehere |first1=Stephen |url=https://archive.org/details/goingnegativenoLynn |title=Going Negative |last2=Iyengar |first2=Shanto |date=2016-06-20 |newspaper=The New York Times |isbn=978-0-684-83711-6 |language=en |issn=0362-4331 |access-date= 2016-06-20 |url-access=registration}}</ref> A 2019 study of [[Online advertising|online]] political advertising conducted by a party in the [[2016 Berlin state election]] campaign found that the online-ad campaign "increased the party's vote share by 0.7 percentage points" and that factual ads were more effective than emotional ads.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hager |first1=Anselm |date= 2019 |title=Do Online Ads Influence Vote Choice? |url=http://nbn-resolving.de/urn:nbn:de:bsz:352-2-gcdpivgb9ylg6 |journal=Political Communication |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=376–393 |doi= 10.1080/10584609.2018.1548529 |s2cid=149800615}}</ref>

==History==
[[File:Acgladstone2.jpg|thumb|right|''[[Portrait of William Ewart Gladstone]]'' by [[John Everett Millais]] in 1879, during the Midlothian campaign]]
Political campaigns have existed as long as there have been informed citizens to campaign amongst. [[Democratic societies]] have regular election campaigns, but political campaigning can occur on particular issues even in non-democracies so long as freedom of expression is allowed. Often mass campaigns are started by the less privileged or anti-establishment viewpoints (as against more powerful interests whose first resort is [[lobbying]]). The phenomenon of political campaigns are tightly tied to [[lobby group]]s and [[political party|political parties]].

The first modern campaign is often described as [[William Ewart Gladstone]]'s [[Midlothian campaign]] in 1878–80, although there may be earlier recognizably modern examples from the 19th century. The 1896 [[William McKinley presidential campaign, 1896|William McKinley presidential campaign]] laid the groundwork for modern campaigns.<ref name=Wiesner2014>{{cite book|last1=Wiesner-Hanks|first1=Merry E.|last2=Evans|first2=Andrew D.|last3=Wheeler|first3=William Bruce|last4=Ruff|first4=Julius|title=Discovering the Western Past, Volume II: Since 1500|date=2014|publisher=Cengage Learning|isbn=978-1-111-83717-4|page=336|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bbwTCgAAQBAJ&pg=PT351}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Price|first1=Richard|title=British Society 1680-1880: Dynamism, Containment and Change|date=1999|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-65701-3|page=289|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oqY4cFsaK7AC&pg=PA289|language=en}}</ref>

=== History of election campaigns in America ===
In the 19th Century, American presidential candidates seldom traveled or made speeches in support of their candidacies. Through 1904, only eight major presidential candidates did so ([[William Henry Harrison]] in [[1840 United States presidential election|1840]], [[Winfield Scott]] in [[1852 United States presidential election|1852]], [[Stephen A. Douglas]] in [[1860 United States presidential election|1860]], [[Horatio Seymour]] in [[1868 United States presidential election|1868]], [[Horace Greeley]] in [[1872 United States presidential election|1872]], [[James A. Garfield]] in [[1880 United States presidential election|1880]], [[James G. Blaine]] in [[1884 United States presidential election|1884]], [[William Jennings Bryan]] in [[1896 United States presidential election|1896]] and [[1900 United States presidential election|1900]], and [[Alton B. Parker]] in [[1904 United States presidential election|1904]]), whereas every major presidential candidate since then has done so, with the sole exception of [[Calvin Coolidge]] in [[1924 United States presidential election|1924]].<ref>Bourdon, Jeffrey Normand. "Just Call Me Bill: 
William Taft Brings Spectacle Politics to the Midwest," ''Studies in Midwestern History'', [https://scholarworks.gvsu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1019&context=midwesternhistory Vol.2, No.10, p.114] (Oct. 2016) (retrieved July 21, 2024); Blaine, James Gillespie, [https://ia801303.us.archive.org/6/items/eulogyonjamesabr02blai/eulogyonjamesabr02blai.pdf ''Eulogy on James Abram Garfield'', p.41] (Boston: [[James R. Osgood]] & Co., Feb, 27, 1882) (retrieved July 21, 2024) ("Garfield spoke to large crowds as he journeyed to and from New York in August, [and] to a great multitude in that
city").</ref> In 1896, William McKinley recruited the help of Marcus A. Hanna. Hanna devised a plan to have voters come to McKinley. McKinley won the race with 51% of the votes.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Burton|first1=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Bf7WCQAAQBAJ&q=political+campaign&pg=PP1|title=Campaign Craft: The Strategies, Tactics, and Art of Political Campaign Management, 5th Edition: The Strategies, Tactics, and Art of Political Campaign Management|last2=Miller|first2=William J.|last3=Shea|first3=Daniel M.|date=2015-06-23|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-4408-3733-3|language=en}}</ref>

The development of new technologies has completely changed the way political campaigns are run. In the late 20th Century, campaigns shifted into television and radio broadcasts. The early 2000s brought interactive websites. By 2008 the world of campaigns was available to millions of people through the internet and social media programs. 2008 marked a new era of digital elections because of the fast-paced movement of information.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Owen|first=Diana|editor2-first=Kathleen Hall|editor2-last=Jamieson|editor1-first=Kate|editor1-last=Kenski|date=2017-08-24|title=New Media and Political Campaigns|url=https://www.oxfordhandbooks.com/view/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780199793471.001.0001/oxfordhb-9780199793471-e-016|access-date=2020-12-01|website=The Oxford Handbook of Political Communication|language=en|doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780199793471.001.0001|isbn=978-0-19-979347-1}}</ref>{{Clear}}

==See also==
{{Portal|Politics}}
;Techniques and traditions
*[[Assumed Incumbency]]
*[[Artificial intelligence and elections]] - Use of AI in elections and political campaigning.

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

==Sources==

===World===
*{{cite journal | last1 = Abizadeh | first1 = Arash | year = 2005 | title = Democratic Elections without Campaigns? Normative Foundations of National Baha'i Elections | url = http://igen-networks.com/democratic-elections-without-campaigns/ | journal = World Order | volume = 37 | issue = 1| pages = 7–49 }}
*Barnes, S. H., and M. Kaase ''Political Action: Mass Participation in Five Western Democracies''. Sage, 1979.
*Blewett, Neal. ''The Peers, the Parties and the People: The General Elections of 1910''. London: Macmillan, 1972.
*Hix, S. ''The Political System of the European Union''. St. Martin's Press, 1999.
*Katz, Richard S., and [[Peter Mair]] (eds.), ''How Parties Organize: Change and Adaptation in Party Organizations in Western Democracies''. Sage Publications, 1994.
*{{cite journal | last1 = Katz | first1 = Richard S. | last2 = Mair | first2 = Peter | year = 1995| title = Changing Models of Party Organization and Party Democracy: The Emergence of the Cartel Party | journal = Party Politics | volume = 1| pages = 5–28| doi = 10.1177/1354068895001001001 | s2cid = 143611762 }}
*LaPalombara, Joseph and Myron Wiener (eds.), ''Political Parties and Political Development''. Princeton University Press, 1966.
*Panebianco, A. ''Political Parties: Organization and Power''. Cambridge University Press, 1988.
*Paquette, Laure. ''Campaign Strategy''. New York: Nova, 2006.
*Poguntke, Thomas, and Paul Webb, eds. ''The Presidentialization of Politics: A Comparative Study of Modern Democracies''. [https://www.questia.com/PM.qst?a=o&d=110133557 Oxford University Press. 2005 online] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110418185441/http://www.questia.com/PM.qst?a=o&d=110133557 |date=2011-04-18 }}
*Ware, Alan. ''Citizens, Parties and the State: A Reappraisal''. Princeton University Press, 1987.
*Webb, Paul, David Farrell, and Ian Holliday, [http://igen-networks.com/PM.qst?a=o&d=109863433 ''Political Parties in Advanced Industrial Democracies'']. Oxford University Press, 2002

===United States===
* Bruce A. Bimber and Richard Davis, ''Campaigning Online: the Internet in U.S. Elections''. Oxford University Press, 2003.
* Cunningham, Sean P. ''Cowboy Conservatism: Texas and the Rise of the Modern Right''. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2010.
* Robert J. Dinkin. ''Campaigning in America: A History of Election Practice''. Westport: Greenwood, 1989.
* Kirsten A. Foot and Steven M. Schneider, [https://archive.today/20060915053642/http://mitpress.mit.edu/webcampaigning ''Web Campaigning'']. The MIT Press, 2006.
* John Gerring, ''Party Ideologies in America, 1828–1996''. New York: Cambridge University Press, 1998.
* Lewis L. Gould, ''Grand Old Party: A History of the Republicans''. New York: Random House, 2003.
* Justin A. Gravely. ''Campaigning on American Soil and the Rules of the American Government''. Cambridge University Press, 2014
* Gary C. Jacobson. ''The Politics of Congressional Elections'' (5th edition). New York: Longman, 2000.
* Richard Jensen, ''The Winning of the Midwest: Social and Political Conflict, 1888–1896''. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1971.
* L. Sandy Meisel, ed. ''Political Parties and Elections in the United States: An Encyclopedia''. New York: Garland, 1991.
* Arthur M. Schlesinger Jr., ed. ''History of American Presidential Elections''. 4 vols. New York: Chelsea House, 1971.
* James A. Thurber, ''Campaigns and Elections American Style'' (2nd edition). New York: Westview Press, 2004.

== Further reading ==
* [http://www.annualreviews.org/doi/abs/10.1146/annurev-polisci-072012-113556?journalCode=polisci Gary C. Jacobson. 2015. How Do Campaigns Matter? ]. ''[[Annual Review of Political Science]]''.
